page: Alibaba Cloud ecs.se1ne.2xlarge is an x86_64 architecture memory-optimized server instance powered by an Intel Xeon 8269CY processor running at 2.5 GHz. This instance features 8 dedicated physical cores with 1 thread per core, providing 8 vCPUs. It is equipped with 64.0 GB of RAM, yielding a high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 8.0 GB per core. The CPU includes a 35 MB L3 cache and supports instruction sets such as AVX, AVX-512F, AES, and FMA. No local storage or GPU hardware is bundled with this instance, and it operates on a KVM hypervisor with a baseline network bandwidth of 2 Gbps.

Benchmark data indicates that the processor delivers modest computational performance. Single-core and multi-core stress-ng tests position the CPU in the bottom 25%, while Geekbench single-core scores fall into the bottom 10%. Memory bandwidth performance is more resilient, with read and write operations on large blocks registering in the middle 50% tier. However, database operations and Redis SET benchmarks show weaker performance, placing in the bottom 25%. Small-model LLM inference achieves average throughput, but performance drops significantly when processing medium-sized models.

Given its high memory density relative to its processing power, the ecs.se1ne.2xlarge offers qualitative cost efficiency for workloads that require substantial memory capacity but do not demand high-performance CPU throughput. The lack of local storage requires reliance on network-attached storage, which aligns with its 2 Gbps baseline network bandwidth. This profile is suitable for memory-intensive applications with moderate processing requirements, such as caching layers, lightweight database hosting, and memory-bound enterprise applications.
description: Alibaba Cloud ecs.se1ne.2xlarge is an x86_64 memory-optimized server instance featuring 8 dedicated Intel Xeon 8269CY vCPUs running at 2.5 GHz and 64.0 GB of RAM. Operating on a KVM hypervisor, this instance provides a high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 8.0 GB per core but does not include local storage or GPU accelerators. Network connectivity is limited to a baseline bandwidth of 2 Gbps. Performance benchmarks show average memory bandwidth but weak overall CPU compute capabilities, with stress-ng and Geekbench scores falling in the bottom 25% and 10% respectively. Consequently, this instance is qualitatively cost-efficient for memory-bound workloads with low-to-moderate processing demands. It is best suited for caching layers, lightweight database hosting, and memory-intensive enterprise applications that do not require high-performance sequential processing or local NVMe storage.
